Threat.value: Suddenly feeling or acting as if the stressful experience were actually happening again

Memory.value: When I remember the frightening event I feel like it is happening right now.
My memories of the frightening event are like a film that plays over and over.
My memories of the frightening event are very clear and detailed.

Negative Appraisals.value: I can't rely on other people
I can't trust that I will do the right thing
My reactions since the event mean that I am going crazy

Maladaptive Strategies.value: try hard not think about the trauma
Talking with others about the event or its aftermath
Being reminded of the event by others

issues.item: Sexual Objectification: Being looked at sexually
issues.explanation: Implausible trigger for bereavement trauma in a 56-year-old female. Sexual objectification is typically associated with sexual trauma, not loss of a loved one, and creates cross-node inconsistency with other items that don't reference sexual threat.

issues.item: Bodily Reenactment: When memories come to mind of what happened, I feel my body is in the same position as when the frightening event occurred.
issues.explanation: Clinically unusual for bereavement trauma. Bodily reenactment typically occurs with physical/assault traumas where body positioning was salient, not with loss of a loved one through death.

issues.item: Stay Near Exit: make sure to stay close to exit
issues.explanation: Implausible safety behavior for bereavement trauma. This strategy addresses immediate physical threat/escape needs, which doesn't align with the nature of loss trauma or other reported symptoms.

issues.item: Nonverbal Memory: I can't seem to put the frightening event into words.
issues.explanation: Directly contradicts 'Event Recall: Talking about the event' in Triggers node. Patient cannot simultaneously be unable to verbalize the event while reporting that talking about the event is a trigger.

issues.item: Nonverbal Memory: I can't seem to put the frightening event into words.
issues.explanation: Directly contradicts 'Avoid Talking: Talking with others about the event or its aftermath' in Maladaptive Strategies. Avoidance of talking implies capacity to verbalize but avoidance behavior, whereas nonverbal memory suggests inability to verbalize at all.

issues.item: Downward Comparison: I remind myself it could have been worse
issues.explanation: Clinically implausible with PCL-5 score of 78 (severe PTSD). This cognitive strategy is more typical of mild to moderate distress, not extreme severity where patient fears going crazy and has intense flashbacks.

vignette:
Mrs. A, a 56-year-old woman of South East Asian heritage, presented with significant distress following the sudden bereavement of a close family member. Her life is now dominated by a pervasive sense of threat. She reports intense flashbacks where she feels the loss is happening all over again, and she struggles with profound insomnia and an inability to concentrate. These symptoms of hyperarousal are frequently ignited by specific triggers: returning to the physical location associated with the loss, hearing others argue, or even the simple act of someone talking about the event.

When these triggers occur, they do more than provoke anxiety; they forcefully activate her memory of the trauma. Her recollections are not mere thoughts but are experienced as a vivid, detailed film replaying in her mind, making her feel as if she is reliving the event in the present moment. This relentless, intrusive memory replay is a core source of her ongoing distress.

The nature of her memories has profoundly shaped how she views herself and the world. She is tormented by the belief that her intense reactions mean she is "going crazy." She has come to feel she cannot trust herself to do the right thing and, more broadly, that she cannot rely on other people. These negative appraisals are not passive observations; they actively fuel her sense of imminent threat and color her traumatic memories with layers of fear and self-doubt.

In response to this overwhelming internal landscape—the constant threat, the intrusive memories, and her distressing beliefs—Mrs. A has adopted rigid coping strategies. She tries hard to suppress any thoughts of the trauma and actively avoids conversations about it or any reminders others might bring up. This avoidance and cognitive suppression are her primary means of managing the distress generated by her memories and her appraisals. Consequently, she has become increasingly isolated within her relationship, trapped in a cycle where her efforts to control her internal experience only serve to maintain her PTSD symptoms.
